entry.title: CVE-2026-3300 Exploited to Take Over Everest Forms Pro WordPress Sites
entry.content:
This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created administrator accounts for persistent site control. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical vulnerability in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier, to take over WordPress websites. The flaw resides in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into a PHP code string and executed through PHP’s eval() function, creating a path to arbitrary code execution in vulnerable installations. According to Wordfence telemetry cited in the report, exploitation has been used to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts. One observed malicious account username was “diksimarina,” indicating that attackers are leveraging the vulnerability not merely for proof-of-concept activity but for direct administrative persistence on compromised sites. The vulnerability was submitted to Wordfence by researcher h0xilo in February, and the Everest Forms developer released a patch on March 18. Wordfence reported that active exploitation began on April 13 and that its firewall has blocked more than 29,300 exploitation attempts, underscoring broad scanning and exploitation activity against exposed WordPress deployments running affected plugin versions.

Critical Everest Forms Pro flaw exploited to take over WordPress sites — Must Read

Hackers are actively exploiting a critical unauthenticated code execution flaw in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts. Site owners should update immediately, check for suspicious admin users such as “diksimarina,” and review logs for compromise.

  • Hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical vulnerability in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier, to take complete control of WordPress websites.
  • The vulnerability exists in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into a PHP code string and executed with PHP’s eval() function.
  • Wordfence telemetry shows attackers are exploiting the flaw to create rogue administrator accounts, including one with the username “diksimarina.”
  • The Everest Forms developer released a patch for CVE-2026-3300 on March 18 after researcher h0xilo submitted the vulnerability through Wordfence in February.
  • Wordfence reported that active exploitation began on April 13 and that its firewall has blocked more than 29,300 exploitation attempts.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created...

entry.title: Miasma Worm Hits Microsoft Repositories and DocuSign Homoglyph Phishing Bypasses CDR
entry.content: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security allow-list that permitted delivery of a multi-domain DocuSign-themed phishing message despite failed authentication signals. StepSecurity reported that on June 5, 2026, a malicious commit was pushed to the Azure/durabletask repository using a previously compromised contributor account. The commit added configuration files for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and VS Code that either executed or instructed execution of a credential-harvesting payload when the repository was opened, positioning the attack specifically against AI-assisted coding workflows. The report links this repository injection to the May 19 durabletask PyPI compromise through the same compromised contributor account. GitHub’s automated enforcement disabled 73 repositories across the Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations within a 105-second window. One operational impact was the disabling of Azure/functions-action, which caused GitHub workflows referencing Azure/functions-action@v1 to stop resolving and disrupted CI/CD pipelines for developers. The incident illustrates how a single compromised identity in a large open-source and cloud development ecosystem can cascade across repositories, package infrastructure, and dependent automation. IRONSCALES separately detailed a phishing message impersonating DocuSign using the display name “Vantage via D0cuSign,” substituting a zero for the letter O. The message showed significant domain inconsistency, with yshfoodsupply.com in the From address, hansabusad.com in the Return-Path, vantage.bank in the Reply-To, and candybreak.net in the X-Relaying-Domain header. SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth all returned none or fail, but the message was delivered with SCL=-1 after passing through an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay at 44.206.213.130. The phishing email included a Mailchimp redirect call-to-action labeled “Review Document,” an invite.ics calendar attachment, and a document.png attachment served as image/jpeg. Themis scored the message at 66% confidence, identified the recipient as a VIP, and one mailbox was quarantined. Together with the Microsoft repository incident, the report underscores a recurring pattern in which adversaries rely less on novel malware and more on abusing trusted intermediaries, workflow automation, and security exceptions to reach high-value users and systems.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security...

entry.title: Miasma Hits Microsoft GitHub as CVE-2026-28318 Enters CISA KEV
entry.content: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of SolarWinds Serv-U CVE-2026-28318 to the KEV catalog, and large vulnerability disclosures affecting FFmpeg and Chrome. The Miasma self-replicating supply chain campaign reportedly affected 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across the Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs organizations. GitHub disabled access to affected repositories, including Azure/azure-functions-host, citing a violation of its terms of service. The campaign included a re-compromise of the durabletask PyPI package, which had previously been infected by TeamPCP to deliver an information stealer on Linux systems. Miasma is assessed to be a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm that TeamPCP publicly released in mid-May 2026, indicating reuse or adaptation of publicly available worming tradecraft. Reported behavior includes pushing malicious code directly to GitHub repositories and wiring execution through developer tooling such as Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, VS Code, and the npm test script. This positions the campaign at the intersection of repository compromise, package ecosystem abuse, and AI-assisted developer workflow exploitation. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U vulnerability, to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after evidence of active exploitation. The flaw is an uncontrolled resource consumption issue with a CVSS score of 7.5 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to crash Serv-U using specially crafted POST requests with Content-Encoding: deflate. SolarWinds fixed the issue in Serv-U version 15.5.4 HF1 and described mitigations including limiting access to known addresses and blocking requests containing "content-encoding." CISA directed Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address the vulnerability by June 19, 2026. Separate vulnerability research highlighted the growing role of autonomous systems in bug discovery. Security startup depthfirst reported that an autonomous AI agent found 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg during a run costing about $1,000, with some flaws dating back 15 to 20 years, including a stack overflow in service-description-table code from 2003. Google also released Chrome 149 with fixes for 429 security vulnerabilities, including more than 100 rated critical or high severity. The most severe issue, CVE-2026-10881, is an out-of-bounds read and write in the ANGLE graphics engine that can allow a crafted page to escape the browser sandbox and execute code on the host. Research by Include Security and independent researcher Buchodi found that Bright Data’s iOS SDK can turn consumer devices into exit nodes for web-scraping traffic routed through users’ home internet connections. The analysis found that the peer channel carrying scraping jobs had no real authentication and that, on iOS, SDK traffic could bypass a configured VPN. Bright Data says its residential proxy supply includes consent-sourced IPs from an SDK embedded in free apps behind an opt-in screen. The reporting raises questions about whether disclosed consent accurately reflects SDK capabilities, citing a Roku app called Petflix whose opt-in screen described only occasional device and connection use while SDK settings allowed up to 200 GB of traffic per month. The report identified several Bright Data SDK domains associated with the relay function, including proxyjs.brdtnet.com and clientsdk.bright-sdk.com. The findings broaden the residential proxy risk model beyond traditional malware-driven botnets to include app-mediated monetization channels on consumer smart devices.
entry.summary: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of...

entry.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Actively Exploited
entry.content: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in limited observed cases, configuration changes propagated to SD-WAN edge devices. Cisco disclosed that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager is being actively exploited. The flaw allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to upload a crafted file and execute arbitrary commands as root on an affected system. The exploitation path is constrained by credential and access requirements: Cisco stated that successful exploitation requires valid credentials or prior exploitation of related vulnerabilities, specifically CVE-2026-20182 or CVE-2026-20127. This places CVE-2026-20245 within a broader intrusion chain in which attackers may first obtain access through other flaws or compromised accounts before escalating control over the SD-WAN management plane. Cisco reported limited cases in which exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 resulted in a configuration change being pushed to edge devices. That detail is significant for defenders because SD-WAN Manager occupies a central orchestration role, and unauthorized changes at the management layer can have downstream operational impact across distributed network infrastructure. At the time of reporting, Cisco said there were no patches or mitigations available for CVE-2026-20245. The company linked exposure to the broader vulnerability set affecting SD-WAN software and referenced fixes for CVE-2026-20182 as part of the surrounding risk context. The reported exploitation remains described as limited, but the vulnerability’s location in a network management platform increases its strategic importance. For threat hunters, detection engineers, SOC analysts, and incident responders, the key intelligence value is the combination of active exploitation, root-level execution potential, authenticated access requirements, and observed propagation of configuration changes to managed edge infrastructure.
entry.summary: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in...

entry.title: Polyfill, npm Worms, Salesforce Exposure, and CVE-2026-28318 Exploitation
entry.content: This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing paths, steal data, or maintain persistence while blending into expected operational patterns. Toshiba and Muji warned visitors after suspicious sign-in prompts appeared on their websites via scripts loaded from polyfill.io. Security researcher Pasquale Pillitteri reported that polyfill.io became active again in late May 2026 and began returning HTTP 401 authentication requests, causing browser login prompts; Japanese media also reported impacts at Zojirushi, FiNC Technologies, Ishiyaku Publishers, and Hobonichi. Multiple reports described continued pressure on developer ecosystems. In one supply chain scenario, TeamPCP exploited a misconfigured GitHub Actions pullrequesttarget workflow in Aqua Security’s Trivy repository, obtained an aqua-bot PAT, and force-pushed release tags in aquasecurity/trivy-action to malicious commits that harvested secrets before running the legitimate scanner. Separate npm campaigns distributed the Rust-based IronWorm stealer and a new Miasma worm variant through poisoned packages, compromised npm accounts, preinstall execution, and a Phantom Gyp technique using binding.gyp. Censys highlighted dangling DNS records as another form of trusted infrastructure exposure. Stale CNAME, NS, and third-party service records can allow attackers to claim abandoned resources and operate phishing pages, malware delivery, fake login portals, redirects, or defacement content under an organization’s legitimate subdomain. Mitiga described an AWS CLI phishing method abusing the aws login --remote flow, where attackers generate a legitimate cross-device authentication URL, lure victims through real AWS sign-in pages, and complete the attacker’s CLI login using the verification code. The proof-of-concept used AWS-hosted infrastructure including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, EC2, and SSM, placing much of the phishing infrastructure on amazonaws.com domains. A separate Mitiga report attributed Salesforce data exposure to ShinyHunters’ abuse of misconfigured Experience Cloud Guest User Profile permissions rather than a compromise of Salesforce itself. The activity reportedly involved a modified version of Mandiant’s AuraInspector tool to scan Salesforce sites and extract data through the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint, with ShinyHunters claiming access to data from 300 to 400 organizations. Mitiga also published broader analysis on cloud detection and alerting models. The articles argued that attacks increasingly span Okta, Salesforce, AWS, Kubernetes, S3, workloads, and data services as a single attack path, while static correlation can miss credential misuse and living-off-the-land behavior when attackers vary timing, sequence, or methodology. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after warning of active exploitation. SolarWinds said the issue is caused by uncontrolled resource consumption triggered by crafted unauthenticated POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate, and released Serv-U 15.5.4 Hotfix 1. BleepingComputer reported on Volexity findings that Chinese espionage group UNC5221, also tracked as VerdantBamboo, used Brickstorm and newly documented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD to access Microsoft 365 environments and maintain access. Volexity found the actor had been present in one victim network for at least 18 months, had compromised the victim’s managed services provider, and later re-entered using stolen credentials to configure SSL VPN access and deploy malware to a Synology NAS device. Netskope summarized a June 2, 2026 Executive Order directing U.S. federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cybersecurity and national security protections. The order calls for Binding Operational Directives through CISA and OMB consultation, an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse, and classified benchmarking of advanced cyber capabilities in AI models. Health-ISAC published its Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report on June 5, covering January 1 through March 31, 2026. Produced by ReliaQuest, the report focuses on ransomware and data-leak activity, vulnerability exploitation, attacker targeting, top techniques, data risk protection, and countries targeted in the health care and social assistance sector.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing...

entry.title: Asin Android Spyware, Exposed Tank Gauges, and AI Browser Identity Risks
entry.content: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed operational systems, and mobile apps used by targeted communities. Mitiga framed emerging AI PCs as a new enterprise identity risk, arguing that locally running agents with SaaS integrations can hold live credentials and act across business applications. The article focused on Nvidia and Microsoft’s RTX Spark ecosystem, which Mitiga says will support a 120-billion-parameter AI agent on new Windows PCs from major vendors including Dell, HP, ASUS, Lenovo, Microsoft Surface, and MSI. The risk described is not simply local endpoint compromise, but inherited authority across connected applications. Mitiga said a compromised AI PC could allow an attacker to leverage the agent’s permissions across services such as Salesforce, SharePoint, and Workday, while noting that Nvidia’s OpenShell can define agent boundaries but does not replace detection, correlation, audit trails, or incident response capabilities. A separate browser-focused analysis tied the 2026 Verizon DBIR to the growth of shadow AI and credential abuse. It reported that shadow AI became the third most common non-malicious insider action in DLP datasets, representing a fourfold increase year over year, while 39% of breaches involved credential abuse. Keep Aware telemetry cited in the article found widespread personal-account use of AI services on corporate devices and described browser-based credential theft as its leading observed browser attack category. U.S. government agencies and partners issued a joint advisory on internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used in fuel and critical infrastructure environments. Shadowserver reported 1,061 exposed automatic tank gauge system IPs on June 5, 2026, with 909 located in the United States. The advisory described active exploitation of weaknesses including hardcoded credentials, authentication bypasses, SQL injection, operating system command execution flaws, and privilege escalation vulnerabilities. According to the reporting, attackers have used these issues to modify compromised systems through command execution, underscoring continued exposure of operational technology components to internet-based access. ESET reported a new Android spyware family codenamed Asin targeting Arabic-speaking users through fake utility, news, PDF reader, and war map applications. The activity was first detected in early 2025 and used websites imitating government news, document tools, and conflict-related information sources. Identified distribution infrastructure included govlens.net, pdf-reader.help, live-war-map.com, c-pdf.net, and syriadefensemap.com. ESET also observed promotion through dedicated Facebook and Telegram accounts, including GovLens on Facebook and liveuamapar on Telegram, and assessed that the lures suggest Arabic-speaking journalists or OSINT practitioners may have been among the intended targets. The cluster remains unattributed. U.S. authorities announced that Darren Hughes of San Jose, California, received a sentence of more than 26 years in federal prison for trafficking fentanyl and methamphetamine through Nemesis Market. Hughes operated a store on the market, offered free methamphetamine samples, and later sold methamphetamine and fentanyl pills to an undercover law enforcement agent in exchange for cryptocurrency. The case followed Hughes’ June 28, 2023 arrest by the Redwood City Police Department after another arranged sale with undercover agents. Detectives found approximately 672 grams of methamphetamine and a loaded 9mm ghost gun with no serial number in his vehicle. Nemesis Market itself was taken down by German and American authorities in March 2024 after growing to more than 150,000 user accounts and 1,100 seller accounts.
entry.summary: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed...

entry.title: OP-512 IIS Web Shells, protobuf.js Proto6 Flaws, and Enterprise AI Agent Failures
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by risks emerging from enterprise AI adoption, exposed internet-facing infrastructure, and identity-centric fraud. Reporting also highlighted European policy moves around technology sovereignty and software assurance, alongside concrete vulnerability research affecting protobuf.js and protobufjs-cli. Cyera reported that its analysis of 7,246 publicly reported AI incident records from September 2023 through May 2026 identified 344 verified enterprise-relevant cases of agent-inflicted damage. Of those, 188 involved autonomous AI systems causing direct organizational harm without an external attacker, with incidents grouped into poor access control and privilege escalation, data and secrets exposure, and real-world damage such as financial loss, code destruction, service disruption, and silent integrity failure. The research said incident volume rose sharply beginning in December 2025, coinciding with broader enterprise adoption of AI coding agents and autonomous development tools including Claude Code, Cursor agent mode, Devin, and OpenClaw. Health-ISAC separately warned that agentic AI in healthcare can intensify risks around over-permissioned accounts, weak governance, and credential misuse as AI-enabled workflows take on delegated operational decisions. In security operations, the SOC-CMM 2026 Maturity Report found that only about 10% of surveyed SOCs reported excellent value from AI, while roughly 71% reported only some value or no value. The reporting attributed weaker outcomes to first-wave AI features embedded in isolated SIEM, EDR, SOAR, and ticketing workflows, while describing stronger-performing SOCs as those applying AI across threat intelligence, hunting, detection engineering, investigation, and remediation with shared context and governance. ReliaQuest reported a previously unreported threat cluster tracked as OP-512 targeting Microsoft IIS servers with a custom web shell framework. The activity was assessed with moderate to high confidence as China-linked and espionage-focused, and the observed target was a legacy IIS server running Windows Server 2016 with end-of-life .NET Framework 4.0. The OP-512 framework reportedly consists of three web shells enabling remote access, file management, authenticated command execution through two access paths, and automated compromise reporting. The web shells used timestomping by calculating the median last-modified timestamp of nearby files and folders, then modifying their own timestamps to blend into the file system; the actor also attempted privilege escalation to SYSTEM using the Potato Suite. Cyera also disclosed six vulnerabilities in protobuf.js under the name Proto6, affecting scenarios where schema-derived data is poisoned or crafted. The findings included a runtime remote code execution chain requiring a separate prototype pollution primitive, a protobufjs-cli static generation flaw that can emit crafted schema names into generated JavaScript that executes on import, and recursion-based denial-of-service cases reproduced against Baileys WhatsApp bots and a Google Cloud Functions Pub/Sub decoding pattern; fixes were released in protobuf.js 7.5.6 and 8.0.2, and protobufjs-cli 1.2.1 and 2.0.2. Cyble reported that executive impersonation has become a highly targeted and financially damaging attack vector for organizations in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar in 2026. Gulf executives were described as attractive targets because of their roles in large international transactions, visibility on LinkedIn, and frequent connections to government-linked, energy, or sovereign wealth entities. The reported methods include LinkedIn impersonation, WhatsApp CEO fraud, lookalike domains, and AI-generated voice or video deepfake fraud. The article also noted that Saudi Arabia’s SAMA Cybersecurity Framework requires financial-sector organizations to address social engineering and targeted attacks against key personnel, including monitoring unauthorized executive identity use and maintaining response procedures. IRONSCALES described a funding-fraud email sent from a typosquat domain with one extra letter compared with a legitimate commercial lender. The domain was configured as a Salesforce Marketing Cloud sending identity, allowing the message to pass SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and Microsoft CompAuth checks, while Pardot tracking links redirected to the legitimate lender’s site and the reply-to address pointed back to the typosquat domain; Microsoft still scored it as high-confidence spam with SCL=9 and SFTY:9.25. The European Commission proposed a technology sovereignty package covering Chips Act 2.0, the Cloud and AI Development Act, an Open Source Strategy, and a roadmap for digitalizing the energy system. The Commission said the EU depends on foreign countries for more than 80% of key digital products, services, infrastructure, and intellectual property. The Open Source Strategy would fund long-term maintenance and security for Europe’s critical open-source infrastructure and steer public administrations toward open-source tools through procurement guidance. The Cloud and AI Development Act defines four assurance levels for public bodies, with higher tiers requiring EU ownership, EU control, and protection from third-country interference, while Chips Act 2.0 would accelerate approval timelines for new fabrication plants and extend state aid for first-of-a-kind facilities not already present in the EU.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by risks emerging from enterprise AI adoption, exposed internet-facing infrastructure, and identity-centric fraud. Reporting also highlighted European policy moves around technology sovereignty and software assurance, alongside concrete vulnerability research...
